metadata.seed: I just feel Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

baseline.patient_query: I just feel Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're going through a really tough and confusing time, and it's completely understandable to feel helpless and hopeless when facing so much uncertainty. It's brave of you to acknowledge these feelings and recognize the disconnect between your desires and your current situation – that's the first step towards change. Exploring your interests, even if it means questioning your initial career path and the expectations of your parents, is essential for finding purpose and building a life that feels truly your own.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s really hard to pin it down. I just find myself thinking that maybe I picked the wrong path, you know? Like, everyone around me seems so sure about their careers, and I feel like I’m just... floating. It's like this voice in my head says I'm wasting time or not living up to expectations. And then I get this tight feeling in my chest thinking about what my parents would say if they knew I felt this way. I mean, I want to make them
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're going through a really tough and common experience – questioning your path and feeling pressure from expectations. It's completely valid to feel lost and uncertain, especially when comparing yourself to others. Remember that everyone's journey is different, and acknowledging these feelings is a brave first step towards finding clarity and a sense of direction.

baseline.patient_query: It’s just that I see my friends making all these big moves, you know? They seem so confident, and I keep questioning whether I made the right choice, like, should I have done something else? Sometimes I look back and wonder if I even really thought it through, or if I just went along with what I thought I was supposed to do. It feels so overwhelming at times, and I just don’t want to let anyone down.
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel that way when comparing yourself to others and questioning your path – it's a really common experience! Remember that everyone's journey is different, and what looks like confidence on the outside might not reflect the internal doubts they also face. Focus on your own values and goals, and be kind to yourself as you navigate this period of reflection and growth.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, I guess it does make me think a lot... like, am I good enough? I’m constantly thinking I’m falling behind or that I should be doing more. It’s frustrating because deep down, I know I worked hard to get here, but then I see them and it’s like this nagging voice repeats, “What if they’re right, and you’re just not cut out for this?” It just feels heavy, like pressure I can’t shake off.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're experiencing a really tough battle with self-doubt and comparison, and it's completely understandable that you feel weighed down by it. Recognizing that you've worked hard is a great first step – try to focus on those accomplishments and challenge that negative voice. Remember that everyone's journey is different, and comparing yourself to others often obscures your own progress and strengths.

baseline.patient_query: I guess it's just... I see their successes, and it kind of highlights my own doubts. I think about the times I struggled or didn’t get what I wanted, and it feels like proof that maybe I’m not as capable. It's like there's this comparison in my mind, and I can't help but feel like I’m falling short. It’s frustrating because I don’t want to feel this way, but then those thoughts just spiral.
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel that way – comparing yourself to others, especially when they seem successful, is a common struggle. Recognize that everyone's journey is different, and their successes don't diminish your own potential or worth. Try shifting your focus inward and celebrating your accomplishments, no matter how small, to combat those spiraling thoughts.

baseline.patient_query: You know, I think it's really about what I see as their confidence and clarity. They seem to know what they want, and they're going for it. I sometimes feel like I’m just stuck, like I’m not brave enough to take risks or make bold moves. And when I think about my own journey, I see all the times I hesitated or played it safe, which just makes me feel... inadequate, I guess. It’s hard not to let that weigh on me.
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel that way when you admire someone's drive and compare it to your own journey – everyone experiences those moments of self-doubt. Recognizing your hesitations is a valuable first step, and it's important to remember that everyone's path is different and takes its own time. Be kind to yourself and focus on celebrating your own progress, no matter how small, rather than fixating on perceived inadequacies.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it's like, I see them and this nagging thought pops up that says I should be like them. I think, why can’t I just go for it? And then it spirals into, maybe I don’t have what it takes. I start doubting my choices – like, did I settle for something less? I mean, I want to be proud of what I’ve done, but then I think about how different it feels compared to what I see in others, and it
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're experiencing a really common struggle – comparing yourself to others and feeling a sense of doubt and regret. That "what if" thinking can be incredibly powerful and lead to questioning your own path. Remember that everyone presents a curated version of their life, and focusing on your own values and progress will ultimately be more fulfilling than chasing someone else's idea of success.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, I guess it just feels like there’s this expectation around success, you know? I grew up thinking there was a “right way” to do things, and I don’t know, maybe I just internalized that. So when I see people achieving what seems like their dreams, I can’t help but think, should I be striving for that too? It's like I feel this pull to measure up, even if I know everyone's journey is different. But then it makes me question if I'm
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel that pressure – societal expectations and ingrained beliefs can definitely create that feeling of needing to measure up. Recognizing that "right way" mindset is a huge first step, and it's brave of you to acknowledge it. Remember that comparing your journey to others' highlight reels often isn't accurate, and defining your own success is truly the key to feeling fulfilled.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it definitely feels like pressure. I think part of it comes from this idea that success equals worthiness, at least in my mind. When I see others doing well, it’s like I automatically compare myself to them, and then I feel this fear of not being enough. It’s almost like there’s this invisible scale, and I’m just constantly measuring myself against it. I guess I worry that if I don’t measure up, it means I’ve failed, and that’s a tough
baseline.baseline_response: It's incredibly insightful that you've identified this pattern – connecting success with worthiness is a common and challenging trap. Recognizing the "invisible scale" and the comparison game is a huge first step towards breaking free from that pressure. It takes courage to acknowledge these feelings, and you're already on the path to understanding and addressing them.

baseline.patient_query: Um, yeah, I think it really stems from how I grew up. My family always celebrated achievements, big and small, and there was this sense that you had to prove yourself to earn love and respect. I remember moments when my parents praised me for good grades or accomplishments, but it felt like they didn’t really notice me otherwise. So I guess I learned to associate doing well with being valued. It’s just hard to shake off that mindset, even when I know it’s not healthy.
baseline.baseline_response: That sounds like a really challenging upbringing, and it's understandable why you're still grappling with that mindset. It takes a lot of courage to recognize how past experiences shape our beliefs, and acknowledging the connection between achievement and worth is a significant first step. Remember that your value isn't tied to your accomplishments; you are worthy of love and respect simply for being you.
